title: Dockerise Your Laravel App for Local Development (The Right Way)
altTitle: Ditch XAMPP: Set Up Laravel With Docker in 20 Minutes
excerpt: Set up a consistent Laravel local development environment with Docker Compose - PHP-FPM, Nginx, MySQL, and Redis - so every dev on your team runs the same stack.

seo.title: Docker Local Dev Setup for Laravel Apps
seo.description: How to set up a Laravel local development environment with Docker Compose, PHP-FPM, Nginx, MySQL, and Redis - fully reproducible across your whole team.

content.text: It works on my machine is a joke until it's your bug. A new developer joins the team, spends half a day wrestling with PHP versions, MySQL config, and missing extensions, then still can't run the test suite. Docker solves this. Everyone runs the same environment, defined in code, spun up in one command.
This post sets up a proper Laravel dev environment with Docker Compose: PHP 8.2-FPM, Nginx, MySQL 8, and Redis. No Valet, no XAMPP, no homestead. Just containers.
The File Structure

your-laravel-app/
├── docker/
│   ├── nginx/
│   │   └── default.conf
│   └── php/
│       └── Dockerfile
├── docker-compose.yml
└── ... (your Laravel app files)
Step 1: The PHP Dockerfile

# docker/php/Dockerfile
FROM php:8.2-fpm

# Install system dependencies
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    git curl zip unzip libpng-dev libonig-dev \
    libxml2-dev libzip-dev && \
    docker-php-ext-install pdo_mysql mbstring zip exif bcmath gd

# Install Redis extension
RUN pecl install redis && docker-php-ext-enable redis

# Install Composer
COPY --from=composer:2 /usr/bin/composer /usr/bin/composer

WORKDIR /var/www/html

RUN groupmod -g 1000 www-data && usermod -u 1000 www-data

USER www-data
Step 2: The Nginx Config
Step 3: The Docker Compose File

# docker-compose.yml
services:

  php:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: docker/php/Dockerfile
    volumes:
      - .:/var/www/html
    depends_on:
      - mysql
      - redis
    networks:
      - app

  nginx:
    image: nginx:alpine
    ports:
      - "8080:80"
    volumes:
      - .:/var/www/html
      - ./docker/nginx/default.conf:/etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf
    depends_on:
      - php
    networks:
      - app

  mysql:
    image: mysql:8.0
    environment:
      MYSQL_DATABASE:      laravel
      MYSQL_USER:          laravel
      MYSQL_PASSWORD:      secret
      MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD: rootsecret
    volumes:
      - mysql_data:/var/lib/mysql
    ports:
      - "3306:3306"
    networks:
      - app

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"
    networks:
      - app

networks:
  app:
    driver: bridge

volumes:
  mysql_data:
Step 4: Your .env Settings

DB_CONNECTION=mysql
DB_HOST=mysql
DB_PORT=3306
DB_DATABASE=laravel
DB_USERNAME=laravel
DB_PASSWORD=secret

REDIS_HOST=redis
REDIS_PORT=6379

QUEUE_CONNECTION=redis
CACHE_DRIVER=redis
SESSION_DRIVER=redis
The host values mysql and redis match the service names in docker-compose.yml - that's how Docker's internal DNS resolves them.
Step 5: First Run

# Build and start all containers
docker compose up -d --build

# Install PHP dependencies
docker compose exec php composer install

# Run migrations
docker compose exec php php artisan migrate --seed

# Generate app key
docker compose exec php php artisan key:generate

Visit http://localhost:8080 and your Laravel app is running. Every artisan command runs through docker compose exec php - make an alias like alias art='docker compose exec php php artisan' and it feels native.
Common Mistakes

items: Using root as the PHP container user - running as root means generated files (cache, logs, storage) are owned by root on your host machine. The Dockerfile sets www-data with UID 1000 to match your host user.
Mounting the whole project into multiple containers - both nginx and php need access to the project files. Mount the same volume into both services.
Forgetting to add .env to .gitignore and committing real credentials - use .env.example with placeholder values and let each developer copy and fill it.
Not persisting MySQL data with a named volume - without mysql_data, every docker compose down destroys your database. The named volume keeps it between restarts.

content.text: Final Takeaway

A Dockerised dev environment is a one-time investment that pays dividends every time someone new joins the project or you switch machines. The setup in this post runs Laravel, MySQL, Redis, and Nginx consistently across macOS, Linux, and Windows.

Action step: add this docker-compose.yml to your current Laravel project today and test that it boots cleanly. Then commit it. The next time someone onboards, their setup time goes from half a day to 20 minutes.
